area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.1: The four stations V·O·S·J (Vault, Orchestrate, Shift, Jump) are the 'operating surface' where every migration is observable, audited, and metered (Figure 1).
evidence: src/server.js mounts the factory control plane; src/api/routes.js lists waves and workloads with phase state; src/engine/state-machine.js implements the finite-state machine with station-mapped phases (P1-P2 on V, P3-P4 on O, P5 on S, P6-P7 on J via templates/caf.json lines 8-129); public/app.html/app.js displays live waves board with current phase and valid next states; src/ledger/ledger.js provides immutable audit trail; src/server.js /health endpoint exposes workload and wave counts. However, no event-emitter for 'every transition emits an event' (§5.3 Observable claim) — transitions are persisted to the ledger but not broadcast; no metering backend (cost/effort capture mentioned in whitepaper §18 is missing).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.2: Five-layer architecture: Experience Layer (portal, board, template library); Factory Control Plane (V-O-S-J engine, framework compiler, disposition engine, gates, audit/metering); AI Execution Substrate (MCP server + devstation fleet + digital twins); Capability Layer (executors, registry, vault, connectors, reconciliation); Platform Foundation (Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, RBAC, secrets, LLM bridge, CD pipeline).
evidence: Layering is present but uneven. Factory Control Plane: fully implemented in src/engine/{state-machine.js, disposition.js, gate.js, template.js}; src/ledger/ledger.js for audit. Experience Layer: minimal (public/app.html shows waves + 7-R board + ledger console, no template library or portals). AI Execution Substrate: src/mcp/server.js is a JSON-RPC 2.0 endpoint with tools/call (tools.js lists sign_gate, classify_workload, run_reconcile, ledger_verify); however, NO devstation fleet implementation, no per-identity agents, no digital-twin off-hours model. Capability Layer: src/connectors/{demo.js, hyperv.js, azure-arc.js, sdk.js} provide migrate adapters; src/engine/reconcile.js provides verify(). Platform Foundation: src/db/{pool.js, statestore.js} provide PostgreSQL or in-memory backend; src/config.js reads environment; src/api/auth.js implements bearer-token RBAC. Missing: Kubernetes-native runtime (runs as a single Node.js process), LLM bridge (agent invocation is external, not baked into VOSJ), comprehensive secrets vault (config.js reads VAULT_MASTER_KEY but no vault implementation shown).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.3 Cross-cutting property (Observable): 'Every station exposes a live board; every transition emits an event.'
evidence: Live board: public/app.js implements a focused command-center UI fetching /api/waves, /api/workloads, /api/ledger, and /health in real-time via fetch/DOM (lines 93-99, 65-73). Each wave card shows current phase and valid next states. However, there is no event stream or WebSocket endpoint; the board requires manual refresh (button id='refreshAll' in public/app.html line 45). The statement 'every transition emits an event' is not realized: transitions are persisted to the ledger but not published to subscribers/listeners.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.3 Cross-cutting property (Audited): 'Who advanced/cut-over/decommissioned what, on what evidence, source→target, is recorded immutably.'
evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js implements a tamper-evident, HMAC-SHA256 hash-chained audit log (Invariant 4 in the paper). Each transition is appended as an immutable row with seq, ts, actor, signerRole, action, evidenceHashes, meta, prevHash, and hash (lines 52-74). The verifyChain() method (lines 76-94) detects any forgery or back-dating. Canonical JSON serialization ensures reproducible HMACs. The ledger FAILS CLOSED if VOSJ_LEDGER_HMAC_KEY is absent (line 38). src/engine/gate.js collectEvidence() (lines 84-91) hashes proofs and evidence into the ledger. The public/app.html ledger console (lines 76-94) displays the chain with a 'Verify Chain' button calling /api/ledger/verify. Every signed gate is recorded by HumanGateSigner.sign() (gate.js lines 52-81).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.3 Cross-cutting property (Metered): 'Effort and cost per migration are captured, making outcomes reportable and priceable.'
evidence: src/server.js /health endpoint reports counts of workloads and waves (lines 59-60, 46-49) but no effort or cost tracking. src/config.js defines baselineMaxAgeMs (line 66) but no pricing, cost per unit, or effort-per-phase metrics. The whitepaper §18 (Economics & Value Model) describes metering as central to the service model, but the CE implementation has no cost ledger, no per-minute billing, no effort attribution to actors. This is likely intentionally scoped to Enterprise Edition.

evidence: Thresholds: src/config.js exposes baselineMaxAgeMs as a configurable int (line 66, default 24h, via VOSJ_BASELINE_MAX_AGE_MS). Reconciliation thresholds in src/engine/reconcile.js are hardcoded (PRE_SWITCH_CATEGORIES on lines 14-17). Providers: src/server.js registers a hardcoded DemoConnector (line 16); connectors are stored in ctx.connectors.set('demo', ...) but no dynamic provider registry. Regions/prices: no configuration for Azure/Hyper-V/Azure-Arc regions or pricing tiers. src/config.js reads database, auth, and ledger settings from environment but no provider-registry file or dynamic price lookup.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5.3 Cross-cutting property (Flag-gated): 'No half-built surface ships unflagged; closure follows the standard change-management and CD path.'
evidence: src/server.js implements optional module mounting via mountOptional() (lines 65-75): api/routes and mcp/server are loaded with try/catch, and any missing module logs an INFO message rather than crashing (line 72). The health endpoint (line 42) reports as ok: true even if optional modules fail to mount, allowing a degraded but functional startup. Each module exports function mount(app, ctx) before being installed. However, there is no explicit feature-flag system (no database-driven flag toggles, no dynamic enable/disable for individual features beyond the load-time try/catch).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §6 & §7: 'The V-O-S-J factory model ensures that disposition (7-R) is assigned per workload in Phase 2 (Examine) and enforced as a gate input: the kickoff gate cannot pass until every in-scope workload carries a disposition.' (R3)
evidence: src/engine/disposition.js defines the 7-R dispatch table (DISPOSITIONS, lines 12-71) with each disposition mapped to a typed contract <executor_class, runbook_template, reconciliation_profile, cutover_style>. src/engine/index.js exposes disposition.classify() and disposition.contractFor() (lines 50-51). templates/caf.json Phase 2 (P2, 'Examine') lists 'disposition-7r' as a required activity (line 31) and 'Kickoff complete' gate requires 'every in-scope workload carries a disposition' (lines 34-40). The engine validates that every workload has a disposition before advancing past P2 via the state-machine gating. src/api/routes.js allows workload creation with a disposition field (lines 79-99), which is persisted to the store.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §7: 'The structural guarantee: Because Refactor and Relocate dispositions resolve to runbook templates that only emit incremental, parallel-run cutover steps, the highest-risk transformations are physically incapable of producing a big-bang plan.' (§7 structural guarantee)
evidence: src/engine/disposition.js lines 37-44 (Relocate) and 62-70 (Refactor) set cutoverStyle: CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG, which is physically enforced by the contract table. Line 88 exposes strangler and bigBangAvailable as read-only flags. The disposition engine does not expose a runbook-override: the contract is a typed tuple, and the cutover style is selected based purely on the disposition key, with no runtime switch. If a user tries to apply a Refactor workload, the system CANNOT return bigBangAvailable: true (it returns strangler: true). This is enforced by the data structure, not by policy review.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §6.1 & §12: 'Verified-before-Jump is structural: the last Shift→Jump transition is a mandatory gate on every framework template; a template cannot omit it.' (§6.1 callout)
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js lines 9-26 define INJECTED_CUTOVER_GATE as a non-removable, engine-injected gate with id='engine.verified-before-jump', requiresSignature: true, and cutover: true. Lines 17-26 freeze it as a constant. Line 119-120 ensures it is always in the gate index. src/engine/reconcile.js (lines 38-83) mandates that cutover gates require a passing proof.ok === true, and src/engine/gate.js lines 44-50 enforce fail-closed: if !proof || proof.ok !== true, the gate sign is rejected. A template cannot override or delete INJECTED_CUTOVER_GATE because it is injected after template compilation (state-machine.js line 120).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §6.1 & §12: Gates require both machine-checkable criteria AND human sign-off by a named role: 'Machines verify facts; humans accept accountability' (Invariant 1 & 2: no agent self-sign, separation of duties).
evidence: src/engine/gate.js HumanGateSigner.sign() enforces: (1) signer.kind === 'human' (line 42, via GateSigner.assertHumanIndependent); (2) author !== signer.id (line 46); (3) gate.signerRole must match signer.role (lines 33-36); (4) gate.criteriaMet === true (line 40-41); (5) cutover gates require proof.ok === true (lines 44-50). Violations throw errors (fail-closed). src/api/routes.js transitionHandler() (lines 138-150) supplies the human signer in the request body, never from the auth layer; the engine re-validates. src/contracts/index.js lines 33-49 codify this as a GateSigner base-class requirement.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §14.1 & §8: 'The framework template engine compiles any methodology into a signed-phase-gate state machine. The signing, gate-row persistence, oversight roles, and RBAC machinery are reused unchanged; only the state-machine source moves from frozen constants to template rows.' Methodology becomes configuration, not code.
evidence: src/engine/template.js loadDir()/loadFile() read JSON templates from templates/*.json (lines 15-29). compile() normalizes phases, gates, and transitions into a frozen template object (lines 32-59). deriveStates() and deriveTransitions() generate the FSM shape (lines 99-114) from phase sequence. src/engine/index.js buildEngine() calls template.loadDir(TEMPLATES_DIR) once at startup (line 23) and machineFor(templateId) dynamically instantiates a StateMachine from the loaded template (lines 31-33). templates/caf.json is a complete 7-phase framework as JSON (130 lines) with no hardcoded state names in code. The same HumanGateSigner and signed-gate engine process any template's transitions identically. src/api/routes.js buildWave() pins the template version at kickoff (lines 124-125) so a later template edit cannot mutate an in-flight run (§8 backward-compatibility claim).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §9: 'The MCP server is a governed order-and-tool channel under which autonomous agents reach the world. MCP encodes calls as JSON-RPC 2.0 with Streamable HTTP transport.' No legacy SSE; a single POST carries the request and response.
evidence: src/mcp/server.js mount() registers POST /mcp (line 98) as a JSON-RPC 2.0 endpoint. validateEnvelope() (lines 77-89) enforces jsonrpc: '2.0' and method as a string. dispatch() (lines 68-75) routes methods (initialize, tools/list, tools/call) and returns rpcResult()/rpcError(). Lines 46-66 implement Streamable HTTP semantics (no SSE): a POST request carries the method and params, and a single JSON response is returned with Mcp-Session-Id header echoed (lines 103-104). src/mcp/tools.js (lines 1-48) defines an explicit ALLOW-LIST of tools (list_templates, classify_workload, sign_gate, run_reconcile, ledger_verify); isAllowed() (line 58) rejects unlisted tools.

wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §15 & §12: 'Fail-closed credential handling: no agent may sign its own gate; four-eyes change validation; fail-closed secrets; verified-before-cutover.'
evidence: No agent self-sign (Invariant 1): src/contracts/index.js lines 41-49 enforce signer.kind === 'human'; agents cannot sign. Separation of duties (Invariant 2): src/engine/gate.js line 46 checks author !== signer. Fail-closed secrets: src/config.js line 49 (LEDGER_HMAC_KEY) has NO default; line 38 in ledger.js throws if absent. src/config.js line 52 (VAULT_MASTER_KEY) also has no default. src/api/auth.js line 64-66 fails closed if AUTH_TOKEN is not configured in token mode. Verified-before-cutover (Invariant 6): src/engine/gate.js lines 44-50 reject cutover without proof.ok === true; src/engine/reconcile.js preSwitchOk check (line 68) and fail-closed baseline freshness (line 34-35).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §12.1: 'REST API routes are gated with requireAuth; mutations additionally require requireCapability.' Invariant 1 & 2 enforced structurally in HumanGateSigner, not at the API layer.
evidence: src/api/routes.js all routes mounted under /api use auth middleware (e.g., lines 61, 72, 77, 101, 113). src/api/auth.js requireCapability() middleware (referenced in routes.js 77, 106, 113) enforces named capabilities like 'migration:workload:write' and 'migration:gate:sign'. The routes handler (routes.js lines 23-32) wraps all async handlers and fails closed on errors (line 29 returns 400 on exception). Importantly, the sign-off gate identity is supplied in the request body (routes.js line 148 buildSigner), never granted by the API layer — the engine's HumanGateSigner re-validates (gate.js lines 29-50).

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5 (Architectural principle): 'Extend, do not fork: VOSJ productises and unifies capabilities that already exist rather than duplicating them.' Orchestrates existing migration executors (Azure, Hyper-V, etc.) under a neutral control plane.
evidence: src/connectors/{hyperv.js, azure-arc.js, sdk.js, demo.js} wrap point-to-point migration executors as Connector implementations (src/contracts/index.js lines 12-26). The MCP seam (src/mcp/server.js, tools.js) allows external agents to call engine operations without forking the engine. src/server.js buildContext() binds existing migration domain capabilities into ctx.connectors (lines 36-37). However, only a demo connector is registered by default; real Azure/Hyper-V connectors are present but not auto-discovered or dynamically loaded from a registry. There is no 'provider registry' that allows dynamic connector registration (§5 mentions 'provider registry').

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §5: 'A neutral control plane spanning heterogeneous source/target platforms.' (R1)
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js, disposition.js, gate.js, and template.js are platform-agnostic. They do not hardcode Azure or Hyper-V. src/connectors/{demo.js, hyperv.js, azure-arc.js, sdk.js} are registered as pluggable adapters. However, the control plane does not expose a unified 'target platform selection' UI or API for comparing cost/risk/effort across platforms (§5 Vault station mentions 'target platform(s) chosen'). The implementation has no multi-cloud capability registry or template overlays for different target platforms.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §9-11: 'The devstation fleet: per-identity, sandboxed, autonomous coding agents, each with its own credentials, repository clone, and audit trail. Human engineers work live; AI digital twins take over off-hours.'
evidence: src/mcp/server.js provides a minimal MCP endpoint that external agents can call, but there is NO devstation fleet implementation. There is no per-identity agent spawning, no credential isolation per agent, no repository clone management, and no digital-twin off-hours model in the CE. The MCP server is a seam for external agents to call tools, but the agent lifecycle, sandbox isolation, and credential management are missing. This is a reference implementation of the MCP contract, not a full autonomous execution substrate.

area: VOSJ Community Edition §5: Four-Station Factory, Layered Architecture, and Cross-Cutting Properties
wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §6 & §7: Framework enforcement that high-risk workloads (Refactor/Relocate/Replatform) are forced onto the Strangler-Fig path and big-bang cutover is 'impossible by construction' (Abstract, §7 structural guarantee).
evidence: src/engine/disposition.js lines 42, 58, 67 set cutoverStyle: CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG for Relocate, Replatform, Refactor. The disposition contract is immutable (lines 12-71 Object.freeze). src/api/routes.js and src/mcp/tools.js expose classification, which returns strangler: true and bigBangAvailable: false for these dispositions (disposition.js lines 87-88). There is no runtime override or plan mutation that can bypass this. The runbook template selection is strictly from the disposition contract (disposition engine §7 claim 'planning gate selects the runbook template and executor strictly from that disposition').

wp_section: §5 (Architectural Overview): 5.1 (Four Stations: V-O-S-J), 5.2 (Layered Architecture), 5.3 (Cross-Cutting Properties); also §6 (Seven-Phase Gated Framework), §7 (7-R Disposition Engine)
claim: §10 & §11: 'The AI-native execution model: devstations are coordinated by the MCP server as a governed order-and-tool channel. Agents call tools, the control plane enforces gates, and the ledger records every action.'
evidence: MCP server and tools: fully implemented (src/mcp/{server.js, tools.js}, src/contracts/index.js). Governance via gates: fully implemented (src/engine/gate.js, src/ledger/ledger.js). Audit trail: fully implemented (ledger.js HMAC-chained rows). However, the agent lifecycle, agent spawning, credential-per-agent, and off-hours automation are missing. The MCP endpoint is a seam for external agents to call in, but there is no built-in agent orchestration. This is a 'bring your own LLM' architecture, not a full autonomous execution substrate.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: The framework SHALL have seven gated phases P1..P7, each terminating in one or more gates that require machine-checked evidence and a human sign-off by a named role (§6: 'ships a reference framework of seven phases, each terminating in one or more gates that require machine-checked evidence and a human sign-off')
evidence: templates/caf.json defines all 7 phases (P1 Envision, P2 Examine, P3 Engineer the Plan, P4 Establish Readiness, P5 Shift/Execute, P6 Verify & Optimize, P7 Jump to BAU & Learn) with ordinal 1–7. Each phase has a gate object with id, signerRole, and criteria array. src/engine/template.js normalisePhase() validates each phase and its gate. src/engine/state-machine.js listValidNextStates() enforces that transitions require a gate and must pass through signTransition(). The gate object structure with criteria[], signerRole, and requiresSignature=true is enforced in normaliseGate().

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: A gate is a transition that cannot fire until (a) its machine-checkable criteria are satisfied AND (b) a human in a named role applies a cryptographic signature (§6.1: 'a gate is a transition in a finite-state machine that cannot fire until (a) its machine-checkable criteria are satisfied (e.g. "runbook citations resolved, dependency sort acyclic, tabletops passed") and (b) a human in a named role applies a cryptographic signature')
evidence: src/engine/gate.js HumanGateSigner.sign() enforces this dual requirement: (1) lines 29–30 call GateSigner.assertHumanIndependent() which rejects non-human signers; (2) lines 40–42 check gate.criteriaMet === false and throw if criteria are not met; (3) the sign() method only proceeds if both checks pass. src/engine/state-machine.js signTransition() calls this.signer.sign(gate, signer) and will not update state without a successful signature. The gate transition cannot advance without both conditions met.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: The 7-R disposition SHALL be enforced as a gate input (not advisory metadata): the kickoff gate (P2 exit) cannot pass until every in-scope workload carries a disposition, and the planning gate selects the runbook template and executor STRICTLY from that disposition (§7: 'Disposition is assigned per workload in Phase 2 (Examine) and stored on the workload record. It is the single most consequential decision in a migration, and VOSJ's distinctive treatment is to make it a gate input rather than advisory metadata: the kickoff gate cannot pass until every in-scope workload carries a disposition')
evidence: templates/caf.json P2 (Examine) gate criteria includes 'every in-scope workload carries a disposition'. src/engine/disposition.js classify() returns a contract object strictly bound to a disposition key, with properties executorClass, runbookTemplate, reconciliationProfile, cutoverStyle. The machine enforces this in state-machine.js by requiring the gate to pass (with criteria evaluation) before advancing to P3. The disposition contract is immutable (Object.freeze()) once classified.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: High-risk dispositions (Refactor, Relocate, Replatform) SHALL resolve to runbook templates that ONLY emit incremental, parallel-run cutover steps, making big-bang structurally unavailable (§7: 'The structural guarantee. Because Refactor and Relocate dispositions resolve to runbook templates that only emit incremental, parallel-run cutover steps, the highest-risk transformations are physically incapable of producing a big-bang plan')
evidence: src/engine/disposition.js DISPOSITIONS table line 42 (Relocate): cutoverStyle: CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG, line 58 (Replatform): cutoverStyle: CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG, line 67 (Refactor): cutoverStyle: CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG. The classify() function returns { strangler, bigBangAvailable } where bigBangAvailable is false when cutoverStyle === CUTOVER.STRANGLER_FIG. This is a runtime property of the contract object, not a policy. test/invariants.test.js line 152–161 asserts that high-risk dispositions have strangler=true and bigBangAvailable=false. The runbook template selection is strictly bound to the disposition (line 56: 'runbookTemplate: refactor-strangler' etc.), not a choice at runtime.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: Verified-before-Jump SHALL be a structural, non-removable gate on every framework template that cannot be omitted (§6.1: 'The last Shift→Jump transition is a mandatory gate on every framework template; a template cannot omit it. A non-verified cutover is therefore not a policy violation — it is an unreachable state.' and §13: 'Enforced as a mandatory gate on every template')
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js lines 17–26 define INJECTED_CUTOVER_GATE with id 'engine.verified-before-jump', marked injected:true and cutover:true. Line 119–120 in indexTemplate() ensure this gate is always addressable even if a template does not declare it. src/engine/gate.js lines 44–50 enforce Invariant 6: a cutover gate requires proof.ok===true and proof.hash must exist. templates/caf.json P6 gate explicitly marks 'cutover':true, and the description confirms 'The Shift->Jump cutover gate is the engine-injected verified-before-Jump gate'. The engine INJECTS this gate; a template cannot delete it (it is non-removable by design).

claim: The cutover gate SHALL require a passing reconciliation proof (proof.ok === true) with a hash before a human can sign it; no proof or a failing proof SHALL cause the cutover to be fail-closed (§6.1, §13: 'The source serves until the migrated piece is proven equivalent. Enforced as a mandatory gate on every template')
evidence: src/engine/reconcile.js line 69 sets ok = Boolean(result && result.ok) && preSwitchOk && baselineFresh. The proof object is only marked ok:true if all pre-switch categories pass (rows 14–17 list hard pre-switch gates: replication_lag, row_counts, checksums, sequence_identity, constraints, smoke) and the baseline is fresh (isFreshBaseline line 31–36). src/engine/gate.js lines 44–50 reject any cutover gate where proof.ok !== true or proof.hash is missing, throwing 'cutover requires a passing reconciliation proof'. test/invariants.test.js lines 71–85 confirm this: cutover without a proof is rejected; a proof with ok:false is rejected; only proof.ok===true + proof.hash allows cutover.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)

claim: No agent SHALL self-sign a gate; persona service identities SHALL be minted WITHOUT any sign-as-<role> capability (Invariant 1, §12: 'An agent can perform all the work leading to a gate and can request a transition, but it cannot apply the human sign-off that authorises it. Self-authorisation is structurally impossible, not merely disallowed')
evidence: src/contracts/index.js GateSigner.assertHumanIndependent() line 42 checks 'if (!signer || signer.kind !== 'human')' and throws 'signer must be human (no agent self-sign)'. src/engine/gate.js line 30 calls this assertion in every sign() method. No code path grants agents a sign-as-role capability; agents have a kind='agent', humans have kind='human', and only kind='human' can sign. test/invariants.test.js lines 47–56 confirm an agent signer is rejected with /signer must be human/. The struct cannot be manually overridden; it is checked before any signature is applied.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)

evidence: src/contracts/index.js GateSigner.assertHumanIndependent() lines 45–48 reject if 'author === signer.id', throwing 'author cannot self-sign (separation of duties)'. src/engine/gate.js line 30 calls this check. src/engine/state-machine.js line 70 passes gate.actor (the actor who initiated the work) to the signer, and the assertion verifies author !== signer.id. test/invariants.test.js lines 58–68 confirm that making the human signer the same id as the actor is rejected with /author cannot self-sign/.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)
wp_section: §6 (The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework), §7 (The 7-R Disposition Engine), §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §13 (Verification & Reconciliation)
claim: Tamper-evident transitions SHALL be recorded as HMAC-SHA256-signed rows binding actor, role, evidence, and timestamp; the signed ledger SHALL be the authoritative history; transitions cannot be back-dated or forged without detection (Invariant 4, §12: 'Every gate transition is recorded as an HMAC-SHA256-signed row binding actor, role, evidence, and timestamp. The signed ledger is the authoritative history')
evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js class Ledger implements HMAC-SHA256 signing (lines 20–22, 67). Each append() (lines 53–74) computes hash = hmac(key, prevHash + canonical(base)) and stores the row with hash property. verifyChain() (lines 76–94) re-derives the HMAC for each row and detects any forge or back-date (rows are strictly ordered by seq and prevHash links). src/engine/gate.js sign() (lines 53–67) calls this.ledger.append() with actor, signerRole, action, evidenceHashes, and meta including migrationId, unitId, gateId, fromState, toState, signerId. The row is frozen and stored immutably.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)

evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js _key() (lines 34–41) throws 'VOSJ_LEDGER_HMAC_KEY is not set' if the key is missing (fail-closed, no fallback). src/engine/reconcile.js isFreshBaseline() (lines 31–36) returns false if baselineAt is undefined or stale (>24h by default), and reconcile() line 69 sets ok=false if baselineFresh is false, blocking cutover. line 57 says 'not reported (fail-closed)' for any pre-switch category not proven ok. test/invariants.test.js lines 99–134 confirm: missing key throws; stale baseline => ok:false; missing baseline => ok:false; stale baseline blocks cutover even with a shaped proof.

evidence: src/engine/reconcile.js lines 14–17 define PRE_SWITCH_CATEGORIES: replication_lag, row_counts, checksums, sequence_identity, constraints, smoke. area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)

evidence: src/engine/template.js loadDir() loads all .json files and compile() freezes the template immutably with all phases, states, transitions frozen. src/engine/index.js buildEngine() getTemplate() retrieves a template by id. The state-machine is initialized with a specific template at construction time (line 32: new StateMachine(getTemplate(templateId), ...)). Once a migration binds to a template version, the template is not re-fetched; the version is pinned. While the code does not explicitly store template_version on the migration row, the StateMachine architecture ensures the bound template's version is used throughout the run.

area: The Seven-Phase Gated Reference Framework (§6: Gates, Human Signers & the Structural Guarantees)

claim: A devstation is an autonomous, sandboxed software-engineering agent with its own identity, repository clone, model credential, source-control credential, and worker loop; each station behaves like a human engineer's workstation (§10, §10.1).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/devstation.yaml (Deployment with envFrom per-seat Secret), deploy/poc/30-deploy-devstations.sh (per-seat Secret generation + naming), deploy/poc/seat-manager/server.js (credential assignment + pod restart). No worker loop code or agent orchestration implemented; devstations are code-server IDEs + Claude CLI, manually operated or awaiting external orchestration. The 'autonomous' claim is aspirational at CE level—no autonomous work-claiming, execution, or reporting exists.

area: §10: Devstation Fleet (Roles, Lifecycle & Resilience, Cost, Runtime Isolation & Credential Brokering)

claim: The fleet is heterogeneous; stations specialise by role (architect, builder, reviewer, tester, migrator, fixer, deployer), enforcing separation of duties (§10.1).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/30-deploy-devstations.sh generates identical per-seat Secrets (DEVSTATION_WORKER_ENABLED=false, VOSJ_SEAT_MODE=unassigned). Role assignment and specialization are not implemented. The Seat Manager (seat-manager/server.js) only manages credential assignment and scaling; no role-based provisioning, capability scoping, or specialization logic. Separation of duties is enforced at the gate level (§12) via human signatures, not via fleet role assignment.

claim: One station = one identity = one clone = one account, with commit-via-pull-request as the only path that crosses station boundaries; isolated working trees prevent concurrent stomping and silent loss of work (§10, callout).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/devstation.yaml: Recreate strategy (line 40: 'strategy: { type: Recreate }') prevents concurrent pods for the same seat. Per-seat emptyDir volumes (lines 110–115: project, config, local) are unique to each Deployment instance. Per-seat Secret naming (devstation-<i>-env) associates each pod with its own credential. The one-seat-one-identity model is enforced by the cluster's pod uniqueness semantics.

STATUS enum (pending, claimed, done, failed) enforces order lifecycle. The queue is database-backed (when VOSJ_STATE_STORE=pg) and supports concurrent workers without double-execution.

claim: The fleet runs on the operator's own compute and on flat-rate model subscriptions; marginal cost approaches compute cost, not consultant-hour cost. Flat-rate seats carry fair-use ceilings; at the ceiling the fleet queues/throttles or falls back to metered rates (§10.3).
evidence: Cost model is documented (deploy/poc/README.md, config.env: SEAT_MAX=50, DEVSTATION_COUNT=2) and Seat Manager enforces a cap (seat-manager/server.js, line 32: SEAT_MAX). No fair-use ceiling enforcement, throttling, or fallback to metered billing is implemented. The POC scales devstations inline with the cluster's capacity.

claim: A devstation runs model-authored code beside privileged credentials under a hardened runtime class (gVisor, Kata Containers, or Firecracker-microVM via Kubernetes RuntimeClass), so a container escape does not reach the node or neighbouring pods (§10.4).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/devstation.yaml specifies seccompProfile: { type: RuntimeDefault } (line 58) and standard security context, but no RuntimeClass or hardened-runtime specification. No gVisor, Kata, or Firecracker integration. The whitepaper (§10.4) states this explicitly; the POC does not implement it.

area: §10: Devstation Fleet (Roles, Lifecycle & Resilience, Cost, Runtime Isolation & Credential Brokering)
wp_section: §10.1–§10.4: Fleet roles, lifecycle & resilience, cost posture, runtime isolation & credential brokering. Cross-references: §11 (AI-native execution), §12 (governance & no-self-sign), §15 (security architecture)
claim: Long-lived secrets do not live in the execution pod: a broker/sidecar vends short-lived, audience-scoped, per-task credentials (workload-identity federation / SPIFFE-style OIDC for the model seat; short-TTL tokens for source control), so a compromised station leaks at most a narrow, expiring grant (§10.4).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/devstation.yaml uses envFrom to inject CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY directly into the container (lines 73–75). No broker/sidecar, no short-lived token vending, no TTL enforcement, no audience-scoping. Credentials are K8s Secrets (long-lived, mounted via envFrom). The whitepaper (§10.4) describes a 'broker/sidecar' pattern; the CE POC does not implement it.

area: §10: Devstation Fleet (Roles, Lifecycle & Resilience, Cost, Runtime Isolation & Credential Brokering)
wp_section: §10.1–§10.4: Fleet roles, lifecycle & resilience, cost posture, runtime isolation & credential brokering. Cross-references: §11 (AI-native execution), §12 (governance & no-self-sign), §15 (security architecture)
claim: Per-identity segmentation with minimised standing credentials: necessary, but explicitly NOT a claim that code execution and credentials are fully isolated from one another (§10.4).
evidence: E:/apps/vosj/vosj-app/vosj-app/deploy/poc/devstation.yaml implements per-identity segmentation: each pod has its own Service Account (automountServiceAccountToken: false, line 54), its own emptyDir volumes, and its own per-seat Secret. However, credentials are injected as environment variables into the container, not held in an isolated sidecar. The narrower claim ('minimised standing credentials') is acknowledged but not realized.

claim: §11.1: Human engineers operate live during working hours; AI digital twins take over off-hours and during absence, driving the same pipeline through the same MCP channel a human would use. A digital twin is a persona configured with the standards and judgement of its principal, authorised to perform research, design, implementation, and review autonomously, and instructed to escalate only genuine owner-level decisions.
evidence: src/mcp/server.js (lines 1–10): MCP server with tool allow-list exists and is ready to receive orders from any client (human or agentic). src/mcp/queue.js: durable work-queue for claim/execute pattern is implemented. src/contracts/index.js: Connector/Executor base classes define the extension points. BUT: no built-in persona service, no digital-twin scheduling, no off-hours automation layer, no 'escalation' routing. deploy/poc/seat-manager/server.js (lines 1–45): POC seat provisioning for devstations with CODE_SERVER + CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH + ANTHROPIC_API_KEY support, but this is manual credential assignment, not autonomous twin scheduling. The MCP server and queue are the *plumbing*; the *brain* (AI personas + scheduling) is missing. Docs (CORE-IDEA.md line 81): explicitly mark managed AI and twins as 'EE-only'.

area: AI-Native Execution Model (§11): Human–AI Partnership, Execution Moat, Self-Improvement Loop
wp_section: §11.1 (Human–AI partnership across the clock), §11.2 (execution moat), §11.3 (self-improvement loop); supporting: §10 (devstation fleet), §9 (MCP server), §12 (safety invariants), §6.1 (verified-before-cutover)
claim: §11.2: The execution model is the moat. The operationalised combination is an autonomous fleet that does the toil, a governed channel that makes its actions safe and auditable, digital twins that keep the factory running around the clock, and a self-improvement loop that feeds each migration's lessons back into the templates and the agents' memory.
evidence: src/engine/gate.js (lines 1–12, 29–42): HumanGateSigner enforces Invariant 1 (no agent self-sign) + Invariant 2 (separation of duties) + Invariant 6 (verified-before-cutover), and writes HMAC-signed ledger rows. src/ledger/ledger.js (lines 1–5, 34–40): fail-closed ledger with external key custody. src/mcp/tools.js: allow-listed tools (sign_gate enforces human signer, classify_workload, run_reconcile, ledger_verify). src/engine/disposition.js (lines 5–7): Strangler-Fig is structurally enforced for high-risk dispositions. These are the *governance envelope* (the 'safe and auditable' part). MISSING: (1) the autonomous fleet labour (no scheduler, no persona agent loop), (2) the learning loop (no corpus intake, no template delta generation, no agent priors update). Docs (PURPOSE.md): 'CE is bring-your-own-AI... does not include the managed Luca AI personas... EE supplies managed labour + twins + learning loop.' The moat claim is therefore SPLIT: the *auditability moat* (human gate + HMAC ledger) is real and implemented; the *labour-cost moat* (autonomous fleet + self-improvement) is aspirational.

evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js (lines 52–74): append(entry) computes prevHash, builds a base object with actor, signerRole, action, evidenceHashes, ts, and meta, then computes hash = hmac(key, prevHash + canonical(base)). Lines 77–94: verifyChain() re-computes the HMAC for each row and checks prevHash linkage. src/engine/gate.js (lines 53–67): on every sign(), a ledger row is appended with actor, signerRole, action, evidenceHashes, and meta containing gateId, migrationId, unitId, signerId, capability. The HMAC key is fail-closed (§15.2); lines 34–40 of ledger.js throw if VOSJ_LEDGER_HMAC_KEY is not set. This is sound.

A runbook template that tries to emit a big-bang step for a Refactor disposition cannot reference anything but the runbook_template assigned to it, which is 'refactor-strangler' (line 65), and that template (if it exists) would only emit Strangler-Fig steps. This is a compile-time guard: the incorrect runbook either doesn't exist or cannot be selected. The structural guarantee holds.

Lines 53–74: append() computes hash = hmac(key, prevHash + canonical(base)), binding the previous hash into every row, so the chain is tamper-detectable. This is structurally sound.

evidence: The architecture is designed to support this (src/api/routes.js:157 evidence is accepted and bound to gate transitions). However, NO implementation of a diff validator or four-eyes enforcement exists in CE code. The gate accepts arbitrary evidence[] but does not enforce that evidence came from an independent reviewer tool/service. src/engine/waiver.js shows the pattern for gating on a check (soft), but hard validation of four-eyes on material changes is not coded. This is an architectural seam expecting an Enterprise layer above it.

claim: Invariant 4 — Tamper-evident transitions. Every gate transition is recorded as an HMAC-SHA256-signed row binding actor, role, evidence, and timestamp (§12, Inv.4).
evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js:20–21 hmac() uses SHA256. src/ledger/ledger.js:52–74 append() computes HMAC-SHA256(prevHash + canonical(entry)), returns signed row. src/ledger/ledger.js:76–94 verifyChain() reproduces the HMAC and detects forgery. src/engine/gate.js:52–67 sign() collects evidence hashes and writes them to ledger via append(). src/db/schema.sql:59–69 ledger table persists seq, hash, prev_hash, actor, signer_role, action, evidenceHashes, meta immutably.

claim: Invariant 5 — Fail-closed by default. Safety-relevant defaults deny. A credential vault with no key does not fall back to a development key; a stale baseline blocks readiness; a missing verification blocks cutover (§12, Inv.5).
evidence: src/ledger/ledger.js:34–40 _key() throws 'ledger fail-closed' if LEDGER_HMAC_KEY is not set (no fallback). src/api/auth.js:64–66 requires VOSJ_AUTH_TOKEN or returns 503 (fail-closed, not open). src/engine/reconcile.js:31–35 isFreshBaseline() returns false (fail-closed) if baseline_at is missing or stale. src/engine/reconcile.js:68–69 requires all PRE_SWITCH categories to pass AND baselineFresh AND connector.verify()=ok (three-way AND, any missing = fail-closed).

claim: Invariant 6 — Verified-before-cutover. The source serves until the migrated piece is proven equivalent. Enforced as a mandatory gate on every template (§12, Inv.6; §6.1).
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js:17–26 INJECTED_CUTOVER_GATE is engine-injected, non-removable on every template. src/engine/state-machine.js:90–110 cutoverUnit() enforces proof.ok===true before signing (line 95 throws if proof missing or proof.ok !== true). src/engine/gate.js:44–50 sign() gate signing re-validates requiresProof gates need a passing proof. src/engine/reconcile.js:69 ok flag depends on all pre-switch categories passing AND baselineFresh. A cutover cannot proceed without a passing proof.

claim: RBAC and least privilege — Actions are named {domain}:{resource}:{action} and granted to roles; every data route requires authentication and every mutation requires an explicit capability (§12.1).
evidence: src/api/auth.js:122–131 CE_CAPABILITIES — explicit list of mutation capabilities (migration:workload:write, migration:gate:sign, etc.). src/api/routes.js:77 posts require requireCapability('migration:workload:write'). src/api/routes.js:113 transition posts require 'migration:gate:sign'. src/api/rbac.js defines buildRegistry() for config-driven role->capability mapping. src/api/auth.js:87–107 requireCapability() enforces the cap on every mutation. Agents hold 'agent' kind and limited capabilities; no capability to sign gates.

claim: Auditability as a first-class output — Every station transition, access grant, tool call, and cutover is logged immutably with who/what/when/source→target (§12.2).
evidence: src/db/schema.sql:59–69 ledger table captures every gate transition (actor, signerRole, action, timestamp, meta). src/db/schema.sql:95–104 tool_log table captures every MCP tool call (tool, actor, arguments, result, duration, timestamp). src/mcp/tools.js:103–127 audit() records every tool call to tool_log. src/api/routes.js:209–217 /api/ledger endpoint exports ledger rows. However, NO dedicated 'evidence package' export endpoint exists; the whitepaper (§12.6) specifies a control-mapping manifest cross-referencing ledger entries to ISO/COBIT/ITIL/SOC2 controls. The raw data is auditable but the curated 'governance evidence package' as a deliverable is missing from CE.

area: Governance & Safety Invariants (§12)
wp_section: §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §12.1 (RBAC & least privilege), §12.2 (Auditability as first-class output), §12.3 (Framework mapping), §12.4 (Operating model), §12.5 (RACI), §12.6 (Exportable evidence)
claim: Mapping the gate machine to recognised governance frameworks — VOSJ's invariants and gate machine are an implementation of established governance frameworks (ISO 38500, COBIT 2019, ITIL 4, IIA Three Lines, SOC2/SOX ITGC), expressed in code (§12.3).
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js implements a cryptographically-signed gate machine (FSM). src/engine/waiver.js enforces hard-invariant checks (unwaivable) vs advisory checks (waivable). The architectural design aligns with governance framework objectives, but the CE does not explicitly EXPOSE this mapping. The whitepaper's control-mapping manifest (§12.6) — the artefact that cross-references each ledger row to the ISO/COBIT/ITIL/SOC2 control it satisfies — is not implemented in CE. The control LOGIC is present; the CONTROL AUDIT MAPPING is absent.

area: Governance & Safety Invariants (§12)
wp_section: §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §12.1 (RBAC & least privilege), §12.2 (Auditability as first-class output), §12.3 (Framework mapping), §12.4 (Operating model), §12.5 (RACI), §12.6 (Exportable evidence)
claim: The governance operating model in practice — A Gate Council (change-advisory function) is the standing change authority for each migration, with defined roles (Chair, Sponsor, Risk & Control Reviewer, Technical Authorities, AI Fleet Supervisor) and quorate sign-off (§12.4).
evidence: No Gate Council roles are encoded in the CE. src/api/auth.js mints principals but does not define a 'gate_council_member' or role hierarchy. src/engine/gate.js accepts a signer with role, but no validation that the signer holds a Gate Council position. The framework is architected to support this (gate sign-off roles, ledger capture of signer role), but the procedural/operational layer — the standing council, its meeting cadence, its no-go authority — is left to Enterprise tier. Whitepaper §12.4 describes an organizational operating model; CE provides only the technical gates.

area: Governance & Safety Invariants (§12)
wp_section: §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §12.1 (RBAC & least privilege), §12.2 (Auditability as first-class output), §12.3 (Framework mapping), §12.4 (Operating model), §12.5 (RACI), §12.6 (Exportable evidence)
claim: Exception & waiver handling (time-boxed) — A waiver is a signed ledger row of its own kind, citing the gate criterion waived, the residual risk, the compensating control, and the remediation plan. Never a verbal override. Waivers are expiring objects; on expiry the waived criterion re-arms (§12.4).
evidence: src/db/schema.sql:76–92 waivers table exists with expires_at, status, check_class, scope. src/engine/waiver.js:45–50 isActive() enforces expiry logic (row.expires_at checked against now). src/engine/waiver.js:37–42 isWaivable() restricts waivers to advisory checks and rejects hard invariants. src/engine/waiver.js:37–42 HARD_INVARIANT_CHECKS list shows Invariants 1, 5, 6 are non-waivable by code. However, ENFORCEMENT on re-arm is incomplete: the code checks if a waiver is active, but does not implement automatic 're-arming' of an expired waiver on a downstream gate attempt. The waiver can be queried and honored, but no scheduled re-enforcement of a stale waiver is present.

area: Governance & Safety Invariants (§12)

claim: Control testing & evidence cadence — A continuous control-testing cadence (per transition, per wave, 8-hourly, per engagement, periodic) with zero-tolerance counters (self-signed gates, unverified cutovers, fail-open events) (§12.4).
evidence: No control testing loop is implemented in CE. src/ledger/ledger.js:76–94 verifyChain() can re-derive the hash chain (the capability exists), but no automated cadence runs this. No 8-hourly governance digest, no zero-tolerance counter tracking, no periodic control walk-throughs are present in code. This is an operational procedure (§12.4 'Control testing & evidence cadence') expected to run externally (via a Kubernetes CronJob, an external audit tool, or a third-line team). The CE stores the evidence immutably; the governance cadence is deferred to Enterprise/operations.

The procedural RACI in §12.5 (Fleet Responsible, humans Accountable, etc.) is a governance operating model, not a technical control. CE stores who signed what (signer_role in ledger), but does not track the full RACI assignment (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) per activity. This is an organizational/procedural layer above the CE.

Missing in CE: (f) An explicit export endpoint that bundles these into a single audit package. (g) The control-mapping manifest (§12.6 'Mapped, not just stored') that cross-references each artefact to ISO/COBIT/ITIL/SOC2 controls. src/api/routes.js:209–217 /api/ledger returns raw ledger rows; no curated package. This is a delivery/packaging layer, not a data-collection layer.

claim: Baseline discipline — A baseline-drift guard enforces that a baseline older than a configured window automatically blocks the readiness gate and forces re-baselining (§13.1).
evidence: src/engine/reconcile.js:31–35 isFreshBaseline() checks baseline_at against a configured maxAge (default 24h). src/engine/reconcile.js:68–69 ok flag depends on baselineFresh. A stale baseline forces recon to fail-closed; no waiver can override this (it is in the hard-invariant list in waiver.js:23–30). src/db/schema.sql:27 baseline_at column stores the freshness timestamp.

wp_section: §12 (Governance & Safety Invariants), §12.1 (RBAC & least privilege), §12.2 (Auditability as first-class output), §12.3 (Framework mapping), §12.4 (Operating model), §12.5 (RACI), §12.6 (Exportable evidence)
claim: Revocable acceptance — Even after a reconciliation pass, the database authority may revoke acceptance within a short window if drift is detected. Contingency reversal during execution requires a three-way authorisation (§13.2).
evidence: src/engine/state-machine.js allows cutoverUnit() to proceed with a passing proof, but the reversal mechanism (undo cutover within 30 min) is not implemented. src/db/schema.sql has no 'revocable_window' or 'reversal' state. The gate is signed (immutable in ledger), but a per-workload revocation window and three-way reversal authority are not coded. The architecture can support this (gates are mutable until signed), but the specific 'revocable 30-minute window' control is missing from CE.

No 'requires N signatures' logic. test files do not test a post-cutover reversal scenario.
